text: As Large Language Models (LLMs) continue to advance, In-Context Learning (ICL) has established itself as a cornerstone of modern Natural Language Processing (NLP). Under this paradigm, LLMs perform new tasks by making predictions based on a small set of demonstration examples provided within the prompt. Exploiting ICL has become a vital strategy for both evaluating and extending the latent capabilities of these models. In this paper, we provide a comprehensive survey of the current progress and persisting challenges in the field of ICL. We begin by establishing a formal definition of the paradigm and clarifying its relationship to broader machine learning concepts. From there, we categorize and analyze sophisticated techniques for optimizing ICL, including specialized training objectives, prompt engineering strategies, and scoring functions. We also examine diverse application scenarios, ranging from data engineering and knowledge updating to multi-modal tasks in vision and speech. Furthermore, we synthesize recent analytical research to explain the underlying mechanisms of how LLMs learn from context. Finally, we discuss the limitations of current approaches and propose high-potential directions for future research. By offering this roadmap, we aim to inspire further investigation into uncovering the mechanics of ICL and refining its performance for real-world deployment.
